1.definition: common title for a collection of resources. NOTE: Title identifies elements of a series collectively, combined with information about what volumes are available at the source cite.

2.definition: reference from one resource to another - TEST

3.definition: associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification

4.definition: inverse of wasGeneratedBy where the domain resource generated the range resource implying that the domain resource is a temporal event, such as a data processing action, and the range resource is an entity

5.definition: inverse of wasDerivedFrom where the range resource was derived from the domain resource

7.definition: reference to resources that are parts of this resource

8.definition: reference to a master resource of which this one is a part

9.definition: inverse of operatesOn where the domain resource is, or can be, operated on (i.e.queried) by the range resource which must be an agent (i.e. is something that bears some form of responsibility for the operation taking place)

10.definition: an equivalent relationship to srv:operatesOn from a Service to a Dataset

11.definition: part of same structured set of data held in a computer

12.definition: resource is a revision of associated resource

13.definition: associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification

14.definition: part of a set of imagery that when used together, provides three-dimensional images

15.definition: the domain resource was derived from the range resource which must be an entity (i.e. not an actor with agency or a temporal event)

16.definition: the domain resource was generated by the range resource which must have been a temporal event such as a data processing action or a field survey

17.definition: The domain resource was informed by the range resource which must have been an Entity (i.e. not an Agent with agency or an Activity temporal event). This is a looser association than wasDerivedFrom and does not indicate direct derivation, as wasDerivedFrom does.

18.definition: All operations and activities are terminated. The operator has left the site with no intention of returning to the borehole. The borehole is not necessarily plugged.  Similar to CoalLog status "completed".

19.definition: Drilling is completed. Production infrastructure may be installed.  The borehole has not necessarily been abandoned.  Note that the CoalLog "completed" status is more similar to "abandoned" in this vocabulary.

20.definition: Drilling operations have been temporarily suspended (eg, for weather, maintenance, or other reasons).

21.definition: The status of a borehole commencing from the rig being moved onsite to the time that drilling operations are completed.  Similar to PPDM's "drilling", but different.

22.definition: The status of a borehole during which liquids or gas are being pumped into the borehole.

23.definition: Borehole is being used for monitoring parameters such as water level, water quality, fluid pressure.

24.definition: The borehole has been filled or sealed. The extent of seal may vary from just plugged near the surface (eg, many coal boreholes) to plugged in order to prevent the migration of oil, gas, water, or other substance from one stratum to another (ie, PPDM definition).

25.definition: An abandoned borehole that has also been plugged. The extent of seal may vary from just plugged near the surface (eg, many coal boreholes) to plugged to prevent the migration of oil, gas, water, or other substance from one stratum to another (ie, PPDM definition).

26.definition: The status of a borehole during  which liquids or gas are being extracted from the borehole.

27.definition: The status of a borehole that alternately produces and injects liquid or gas into a borehole.

28.definition: A concept or application exists to drill a borehole, but drilling has not yet commenced or been approved.  Similar to PPDM's "location" classification, but different.

29.definition: The borehole site has been fully rehabilitated. Note that this status is included within the PPDM definition of "plugged and abandoned".

30.definition: Borehole operations have been halted for a significant period of time or in a manner that requires significant effort for the resumption of activities. The borehole can be plugged downhole and fluids injected on top of the plug in order to create a temporary seal to prevent hydrocarbon leakage. The surface equipment remains in place. Suspended is never the final status of a borehole.

31.definition: The drilling status of the borehole is unknown.

32.definition: Activities relating to production wells, etc. For example logging activities, recompletion, testing, repairs, maintenance, plug and abandonment and demobilisation.

33.definition: Examples include vibrating truck and explosives.
33.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

34.definition: Clearly defined administrative areas of a political, natural, or regulatory boundary nature, excluding scientific speculative boundaries such as geological boundaries, ecological / ecosystem boundaries etc.  Examples include coastlines (because coastlines define certain administrative boundaries), state boundaries, electoral boundaries, mineral tenement boundaries, petroleum title boundaries and marine protected areas.
34.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

35.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

36.definition: Photographs of a part of the earth's surface taken by a camera mounted in an aircraft for mapping purposes. This usually consists of a series of overlapping vertical photos taken in strips which can form the basis for mapping.
36.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

37.definition: Pertains to both marine and freshwater ecosystems. For example, reefs
37.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

38.definition: Data describing aquifer properties. Porous, water-bearing layers of sand, gravel, and rock below the earth's surface; reservoirs for groundwater.
38.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

39.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

40.definition: Includes sub-surface distribution of metamorphic, sedimentary and igneous rocks and interpreted linear structural features such as folds, faults, lineaments and shearzones
40.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

41.definition: Measurements of ocean depth and the charting of the topography of the ocean floor.
41.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

42.definition: The benthic zone is the ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water such as an ocean or a lake, including the sediment surface and some sub-surface layers.
42.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

43.definition: Stratigraphy based on the paleontologic aspects of rocks.
43.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

44.definition: A borehole is any narrow shaft drilled in the ground, either vertically or horizontally, it includes Petroleum Wells, Stratigraphic Holes, Mineral Holes, Geothermal Holes, Water Bores
44.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

45.definition: A public record of the extent, value, and ownership of land within a district for purposes of taxation.
45.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

46.definition: The use of non-radiogenic dating studies such as U-Pb ratios
46.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

47.definition: A Coal Seam Methane hole is drilled specifically to discover, asses or produce from a coal seam methane reservoir. EXAMPLE UPDATE.
47.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

48.definition: AEM, ground EM, Point located data, grids, derivative grids, algorithms, images, sections, volumes
48.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

49.definition: The density of a material is defined as its mass per unit volume.
49.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

50.definition: Dissolved solids" refer to any minerals, salts, metals, cations or anions dissolved in water.
50.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

51.definition: Satellite and air platform imagery. This includes imagery designed to represent surficial condition of the earth such as Landsat, SPOT, MODIS, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography. Remotely sensed data acquired from earth observation satellites and Sensing Instruments for both scientific and operational applications, such as LANDSAT 5,LANDSAT 7,RADARSAT 1 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR), ERS 2 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR) TERRA Modis,AQUA Modis, NOAA 17, SPOT, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography
51.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

52.definition: An earthquake is the shaking and vibration at the surface of the earth caused by underground movement along a fault plane or by volcanic activity.
52.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

53.definition: Elevation data such as Digital Elevation Models (DEMs).
53.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

54.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

55.definition: Defined areas for the purposes of oil and mineral exploitation
55.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

56.definition: External datasets sourced from other Agencies
56.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

57.definition: This includes for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams and field mapping survey and site information including field geology observations and measurements.
57.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

58.definition: Measurements taken at a field site such as structural, spectral (PIMA) or geophysical (magnetic susceptibility)
58.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

59.definition: Visual observations and photos of the vegetation, landscape and geomorphology at a field site and descriptions of the mineralogy, lithology, alteration of rocks, samples and outcrops at a field site.
59.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

60.definition: Hydraulic conductivity, porosity, permeability, specific discharge
60.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

61.definition: Data related to the chemical analyses and composition of earth materials. This includes information related to the chemical analysis of organic material, surficial, groundwater and pore water samples, benthic chemical analysis, and stable isotope studies
61.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

62.definition: Age approximations of earth materials such as rocks, sediments and minerals. Age data resulting from scientific techniques such as radio isotopes and chemical dating.
62.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

63.definition: Measurements and representation of the earth, its gravitational field and geodynamic phenomena (polar motion, earth tides, and crustal motion) in three-dimensional time varying space.
63.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

64.definition: The study of the origin of landscapes based on a cause and effect relationship. This includes the physical and chemical interactions between the earth's surface and the natural forces acting on it, such as geology, climate, vegetation and relative base level.
64.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

65.definition: Includes seismic, gravity and airborne survey locations. For example, seismic navigation data.
65.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

66.definition: Interpretation using geophysical techniques and data
66.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

67.definition: Data of the physical characteristics of the earth (crust, mantle, core)  such as gravity, magnetics, radiometrics, and seismic. This also includes information relating to basement and crustal studies.
67.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

68.definition: A Geothermal hole is drilled specifically for the exploration, development, appraisal or monitoring of geothermal plays or resources
68.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

69.definition: Including ground, airborne, point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images
69.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

70.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

71.definition: Natural  and human environmental phenomena, whether slow or fast acting.  Information on the natural environment and its interaction with geology, eg waste disposal and pollution such as geological disposal of CO2.
71.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

72.definition: Includes groundwater, porewater and surface water
72.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

73.definition: Hydroacoustic sensing involves "passive acoustics" (listening for sounds) or active acoustics making a sound and listening for the echo, hence the common name for the device, echo sounder or echosounder.
73.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

74.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

75.definition: The distribution and movement of groundwater and dissolved materials in the soil and rocks of the Earth's crust (commonly in aquifers).
75.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

76.definition: Information related to the distribution,  mapping and physical characteristics of surficial waters. Examples include the locations of bores, lakes, dams, canals, sea levels etc,
76.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

77.definition: Infrasound is sound that is lower in frequency than 20 cycles per second, the normal limit of human hearing. The study of such sound waves is sometimes referred to as infrasonics, covering sounds beneath 20 Hz down to 0.001 Hz. This frequency range is utilized for monitoring earthquakes, charting rock and petroleum formations below the earth
77.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

78.definition: Any structure fixed to a terrestrial or marine location to facilitate commercial or daily activities, or the supply of essential services. Examples include processing plants / factories, ports, oil rigs etc. This includes information regarding the facilitation of transporting  people or commodities.
78.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

79.definition: Describing a substance not derived from a living organism and/or not composed of carbon and hydrogen (a hydrocarbon).
79.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

80.definition: Information related to the interpretation of  baseline geoscientific information integrated with field and petrographic observations and measurements. Examples include the interpretation of geological provinces, outcrop and basement geology, 3D geology models, geophysical imagery stratigraphic cross-sections.
80.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

81.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

82.definition: LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an optical remote sensing technology that measures properties of scattered light to find range and/or other information of a distant target. The prevalent method to determine distance to an object or surface is to use laser pulses. Like the similar radar technology, which uses radio waves instead of light, the range to an object is determined by measuring the time delay between transmission of a pulse and detection of the reflected signal.
82.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

83.definition: Magnetic Susceptibility is the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field.
83.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

84.definition: Point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images
84.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

85.definition: Manufacturing is the transformation, by means of a tool and/or processing medium, of raw materials into finished goods for sale, or intermediate processes involving the production or finishing of semi-manufactures. It is a large branch of industry and of secondary production.
85.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

86.definition: Zones of similar mineralogy caused by heat and pressure
86.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

87.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

88.definition: Includes mineral occurrences, resources, deposits, mineral potential, mineral deposit models, prospectivity maps
88.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

89.definition: A Mineral hole is drilled for the exploration, development or resource definition of mineral commodities
89.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

90.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

91.definition: The distribution of plants, animals; natural biotic distribution patterns and habitats for aerial, terrestrial, marine, and other life.BIOME: are distinct ecological communities of plants and animals living together in a particular climate. Scientists classify biomes in various ways, but the major biomes include aquatic, deserts, forests, grasslands, and tundra.?
91.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

92.definition: Optical remote sensing makes use of visible, near infrared and short-waveinfrared sensors to form images of the earth's surface by detecting thesolar radiation reflected from targets on the ground. Different materials reflect and absorb differently at different wavelengths. Thus, the targets can be differentiated by their spectral reflectance signatures in the remotely sensed images. Optical remote sensing systems are classified into the following types, depending on the number of spectral bands used in the imaging process.
92.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

93.definition: Material  derived from living organisms and is made up of carbon-based compounds.
93.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

94.definition: Interpretation of the nature and distribution of outcropping (or near-outcropping) rock units and structures, typically in the form of maps.  These maps may include integrated regolith unit information in areas of non-outcropping bedrock.
94.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

95.definition: The climate of a given interval of time in the the geologic past.
95.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

96.definition: An environment in the geologic past
96.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

97.definition: Palinspastic reconstructions or geological time-slice interpretations
97.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

98.definition: The study of life in past geologic time, based on fossil plants and animals. Chronology of the Earth's history.
98.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

99.definition: Passive geophysical surveys are ones that incorporate measurements of naturally occurring fields or properties of the earth.
99.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

100.definition: Petrographic observations and measurements such as mineralogy, lithology, alteration, mineralisation, metamorphic grade, weathering and grainsize in specimens and thin sections (micro scale).
100.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

101.definition: A Petroleum Well is drilled for the exploration, development or appraisal of hydrocarbons (oil or gas)
101.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

102.definition: Includes thin section, fluid inclusion observations and measurements of hand specimens from the surface or down boreholes
102.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

103.definition: Data related to the physical expression of the earths surface either terrestrial or marine. Examples include marine bathymetric information, seafloor expressions (mounts, slopes, shelf, rise) freshwater/estuarine bed profiles and  Digital Elevation Models
103.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

104.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

105.definition: Porosity and Permeability are two of the primary factors that control the movement and storage of fluids in rocks and sediments. They are intrinsic characteristics of these geologic materials. The exploitation of natural resources, such as groundwater and petroleum, is partly dependent on the properties of porosity and permeability.
105.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

106.definition: Descriptions and polygon outlines of geological provinces of the Australian continent and surrounding offshore regions.
106.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

107.definition: RADAR stands for "RAdio Detection And Ranging". By virtue of sending out pulses of microwave electromagnetic radiation this type of instrument can be classified as an "active sensor" - it measures the time between pulses and their reflected components to determine distance. Different pulse intervals, different wavelengths, different geometry and polarizations can be combined to roughness characteristics of the earth surface.
107.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

108.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

109.definition: Point located data, grids, derivative grids (filtered, etc), algorithms, images
109.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

110.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

111.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

112.definition: Legal boundaries like mining tenements and cadastre
112.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

113.definition: Data on resource production and/or commodity potential or information. Examples include minerals and petroleum potential, oil and gas fields information, resource and production tonnage and grade information, deposit types and mineralisation styles
113.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

114.definition: Physical measurements of rock properties such as density and susceptibility for use in predictive modelling studies such as seismic predictions, geophysical inversions, and groundwater distribution, or engineering work.
114.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

115.definition: Contains the sampling locations of features which were the target for a set of observations and/or measurements.
115.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

116.definition: SEGY, seismic profiles, images, processed data, weak and strong motion from seismographs and accelerators
116.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

117.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

118.definition: Environmental events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as salinity, acid-sulphate soils, coastal erosion, desertification, climate change
118.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

119.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

120.definition: An Anthropogenic feature to facilitate cultural, societal or religious activities eg cemeteries, recreational areas, land uses related to primary industries,  building infrastructure etc. Excludes information relating to transportation.
120.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

121.definition: Similar data to other geophysical types, but in the "spectral geology" space, with many purposes. For example, PIMA and Hylogger
121.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

122.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

123.definition: A Stratigraphic hole is drilled to obtain a record of the Stratigraphic character, composition or property of the rock/soil/regolith units penetrated
123.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

124.definition: Sudden events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as earthquake, landslide, flood, tsunami, wildfire, tropical cyclone, storm surge, severe windstorm
124.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

125.definition: Examples include potentiometric and phreatic surfaces
125.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

126.definition: Information regarding the location, purpose and methodology related to particular surveys of any kind. This includes, for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams. This category can be summarised as metadata regarding any type of survey undertaken or managed within the Organisation.
126.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

127.definition: The theory and practice of classifying plants and animals.
127.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

128.definition: For example, land vegetation - pest and native flora and fauna
128.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

129.definition: Sudden emergency events associated with acts of terrorism causing damage to human life or the economy
129.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

130.definition: Geological datasets developed for a specific purpose, such as "Volcanoes of the World"
130.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

131.definition: Remote sensing of direct temperature effects is carried out by sensing radiation emitted from matter in the thermal infrared region of the spectrum.
131.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

132.definition: Thermal properties of fluids, gases and solids
132.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

133.definition: The shapes, patterns and physical configuration of the surface of the land, including its relief (local differences in elevation) and the positions of natural and man-made features.
133.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

134.definition: Data relating to any infrastructure facilitating the movement of people or goods by motorised means. This includes roads, railways and flight paths, aerodromes etc.
134.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

135.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

136.definition: Information relating to the supply or provision of utility services such as electricity, gas, steam, water or sewage. Examples include oil , gas , electricity and water pipelines.
136.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

137.definition: A Water Bore is drilled specifically for the exploration, extraction or monitoring of groundwater resources
137.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

138.definition: Reflected waves can be generated from a planar boundary in seismic wave speed that can be recorded at the Earth's surface
138.schemeLabel: Legacy GA Corporate Data Classification Scheme

141.definition: The recorded date is the earliest known date, but it is possible the true date is earlier than what is recorded.

142.definition: The recorded date is the latest known date, but it is possible the true date is later than what is recorded.

144.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:1 000 000 scale TEST

145.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:100 000 scale

146.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:25 000 scale

147.definition: Location estimated from an orthophoto at 1:25 000 scale

148.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:250 000 scale

149.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:50 000 scale

150.definition: Location estimated from a printed map at 1:500 000 scale

151.definition: Location estimated from an aerial photograph that has not been rectified to a known geographical datum or projection

152.definition: Location is estimated from an astronomical observation

153.prefLabel: astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes
153.definition: Location is estimated from an astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes.

154.definition: The location is supplied by a private company. The location method is not known.

155.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation

156.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes
156.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes

157.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS
157.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS

158.prefLabel: dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and stand-alone GPS
158.definition: Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and stand alone GPS

159.definition: Location estimated from a digital elevation model or image

160.definition: Location estimated from a 9 second resolution digital elevation model or image

161.definition: Location estimated from a 90 metre resolution digital elevation model or image of data from the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission

162.definition: Location estimated from averaging a number of Global Positioning System observations

163.definition: Location estimated using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.

164.definition: Location determined using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.

165.definition: Location estimated from a Global Positioning System satellite navigation system

166.prefLabel: GPS observation subject to union all selective availability
166.definition: Location estimated from a Global Positioning System prior to May 2000 before when the US government commonly degraded the accuracy of GPS signals by adding intentional, time varying errors of up to 100 meters to the publicly available navigation signal.

167.definition: Location estimated from GPS observation with P-Code access

168.definition: Location estimated from a printed topographic, geological, or other map

169.definition: Location estimated from an aerial photograph geometrically corrected ("orthorectified") such that the scale across the image is uniform

170.definition: The location method is known, but is not specified in this vocabulary

171.definition: The location is supplied via personal communication.  The location method is not known.

172.definition: The location is taken from a published report or article.  The location method is not known.

174.prefLabel: satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation
174.definition: Location estimated from satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation

175.definition: Location estimated from a satellite image

176.definition: Location estimated from a satellite image provided by Google Earth or Google Maps

177.prefLabel: State or NT agency supplied location, method unknown
177.definition: The location is supplied by a State or Territory government agency. The location method is not known.

178.definition: Location estimated using survey from ground control

179.definition: The location method is unknown. However, the correct value probably exists.

180.definition: The location is taken from an unpublished report.  The location method is not known.

181.definition:
A gently dipping surface, occurring at the base of a bathymetric high, that is elevated relative to the adjacent seafloor.
Cf. "fan/apron" in Harris et al., (2014).

182.definition: An elevation of the seafloor, often found in water depths less than 200 m (adapted from IHO, 2019). BANKS are lower than SEAMOUNTS and tend to occur in shallower water.

183.definition: A discrete, usually angular mass comprising at least two relatively flat surfaces.

184.definition: A marked and/or abrupt change in slope. BREAK IN SLOPE can be used to define the limit of other features (e.g. SHELF, PLATFORM, ESCARPMENT).

185.definition: A typically elongated, steep-sided bathymetric low that generally deepens down-slope (modified from IHO, 2019). CANYONS tend to be higher gradient and more complex features than CHANNELS and are larger and more sinuous than GULLIES. VALLEYS tend to have a more limited depth range than CANYONS.

186.definition: A line that represents the mid-line long axis of a feature. Contrast CENTRELINE with THALWEG and CREST, which define the lines of lowest and highest elevation, respectively, along an elongate feature.

187.definition: A general term for an elongated bathymetric low (adapted from IHO, 2019). CHANNELS tend to have flatter and lower gradient FLOORS than GULLIES and CANYONS, and usually have more variable cross-sections than TROUGHS.

188.definition: A topographic high of generally conical shape, which may have a truncated peak. CONES have more pointed peaks and are more symmetrical than KNOLLS.

189.definition: A line of highest elevation along a bathymetric high, the lateral position of which can vary longitudinally. CRESTS contrast with CENTRELINES, which define mid-line axes along elongated features.

190.definition: An elongated narrow opening (adapted from Goudie,  2014). Unlike CANYONS, CREVICES do not necessarily deepen downslope, and unlike TROUGHS, they are not flat bottomed.

191.definition: A general term for a closed-contour bathymetric low. DEPRESSIONS vary in scale from small local features to larger basins. They generally have lower gradient sides than HOLES.

192.definition:
A steep slope, separating areas of relatively lower slope (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "escarpment" in Harris et al., (2014).

193.definition: An elevated feature which expands (and typically descends) from a locus to a commonly curved outer margin.

194.definition: A relatively broad and lower gradient feature within a bathymetric low, flanked by higher and steeper gradient features.

195.definition: A narrow break in an elevated feature (modified from IHO, 2019). GAPS are generally narrower than SADDLES.

196.definition: An elongated, narrow and relatively shallow bathymetric low which commonly occurs in parallel or sub-parallel groups. Unlike GAPS, GROOVES are elongated features, that often occur in groups. GROOVES tend to have higher length/width ratios than CREVICES.

197.definition: A steep-sided, low sinuosity, relatively high-gradient channel. GULLIES are typically smaller than CANYONS.

198.definition: A distinct elevation generally of irregular shape, less than 1000 m above the surrounding relief as measured from the deepest isobath that surrounds most of the feature (IHO, 2019). HILLS have more irregular profiles than KNOLLS and their length generally exceeds their height (contrast with PEAKS). They are also smaller than SEAMOUNT, and larger than individual HUMMOCKS.

199.definition: A sub-circular (in planform) bathymetric low with steep walls and a generally flat Floor (modified from IHO, 2019). HOLES generally have steeper sides than DEPRESSIONS.

200.definition: A small KNOLL or MOUND, which commonly occurs in groups. HUMMOCKS are generally smaller than KNOLLS and HILLS.

201.definition: A distinct elevation with a smooth, commonly rounded profile, less than 1000 m above the surrounding relief (modified from IHO, 2019). KNOLLS have more regular profiles than HILLS, and their width generally exceeds their height (contrast with PEAKS). They are also smaller than SEAMOUNTS, and larger than individual HUMMOCKS.

202.definition: A linear to curvilinear topographic feature of positive, negative or indeterminate relief. May be discontinuous with variable relief (positive to negative) along its length.

203.definition: An annular or partially annular bathymetric low typically located at the base of isolated raised features (modified from IHO, 2019).

204.definition: A distinct elevation with a variable, sometimes rounded profile which is generally less than 500 m above the surrounding seafloor (modified from IHO, 2019).

205.definition: A prominent, commonly pointed elevation rising from a larger feature (modified from IHO, 2019).

206.definition: A spire-shaped pillar, either isolated or rising from a larger feature (IHO, 2019).

208.definition:
A generally closed-contoured, relatively flat-topped bathymetric high with one or more relatively steep sides (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "plateau" in Harris et al., (2014).

209.definition: A generally broad, planar surface that is at least partially elevated, and lower gradient, than the surrounding areas (adapted from Goudie, 2014).

210.definition: A protrusion extending from a bathymetric high into deeper water (adapted from IHO, 2019).

211.definition:
An elongated elevation of varying complexity, size and gradient (length > width) (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf."bridge" and "ridge" in Harris et al., (2014).

212.definition: A broad pass in an elevated feature (modified from IHO, 2019). SADDLES are generally broader (relative to their height) than GAPS.

213.definition:
A prominent feature rising more than 1000 m above the surrounding relief (modified from IHO, 2019). SEAMOUNTS are larger than KNOLLS and HILLS (>1000 m) and may incorporate PEAKS (and other features).
Cf. "seamount" and "abyss-mountains" in Harris et al., (2014).

214.definition:
A relatively shallow elevated feature between (or adjacent to) bathymetric lows (e.g. DEPRESSIONS) (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "sill" in Harris et al., (2014).

216.definition:
A generally elongated and planar feature consisting of an upper planar surface, and relatively steeper descending slope. The planar surface is bounded on one side by a steeper ascending slope (modified from IHO, 2019). A TERRACE is not closed-contoured like a PLATEAU, and its lower-gradient surface can be mapped as a PLATFORM.
Cf "terrace" in Harris et al., (2014).

217.definition: A line of lowest elevation along an elongate bathymetric low, the lateral position of which can vary longitudinally. THALWEGS contrast with CENTRELINES, which define mid-line axes along bathymetric lows.

218.definition: A long, deep, asymmetrical bathymetric low with relatively steep sides (adapted from IHO, 2019). Unlike TROUGHS, TRENCHS are typically asymmetric in cross-section.

219.definition:
An elongated bathymetric low, generally wide and flat bottomed with symmetrical and sub-parallel sides (modified from IHO, 2019). Unlike DEPRESSIONS, TROUGHS are not necessarily closed-contoured, and unlike VALLEYS, they do not always deepen down-slope. Unlike TRENCHS, TROUGHS are typically symmetrical in cross-section.
Cf. "trough" in Harris et al., (2014).

220.definition: An elongated bathymetric low, typically occurring between prominent bathymetric highs, which generally widens and deepens down-slope (modified from IHO, 2019). VALLEYS have more limited depth-ranges than CANYONS.

orderedDocs: astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes
dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes
dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS
dead reckoning tied to transit satellite fixes and stand-alone GPS
GPS observation subject to union all selective availability
satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation
State or NT agency supplied location, method unknown
A borehole is any narrow shaft drilled in the ground, either vertically or horizontally, it includes Petroleum Wells, Stratigraphic Holes, Mineral Holes, Geothermal Holes, Water Bores
A broad pass in an elevated feature (modified from IHO, 2019). SADDLES are generally broader (relative to their height) than GAPS.
A Coal Seam Methane hole is drilled specifically to discover, asses or produce from a coal seam methane reservoir. EXAMPLE UPDATE.
A concept or application exists to drill a borehole, but drilling has not yet commenced or been approved.  Similar to PPDM's "location" classification, but different.
A discrete, usually angular mass comprising at least two relatively flat surfaces.

A distinct elevation generally of irregular shape, less than 1000 m above the surrounding relief as measured from the deepest isobath that surrounds most of the feature (IHO, 2019). HILLS have more irregular profiles than KNOLLS and their length generally exceeds their height (contrast with PEAKS). They are also smaller than SEAMOUNT, and larger than individual HUMMOCKS.

A distinct elevation with a smooth, commonly rounded profile, less than 1000 m above the surrounding relief (modified from IHO, 2019). KNOLLS have more regular profiles than HILLS, and their width generally exceeds their height (contrast with PEAKS). They are also smaller than SEAMOUNTS, and larger than individual HUMMOCKS.
A distinct elevation with a variable, sometimes rounded profile which is generally less than 500 m above the surrounding seafloor (modified from IHO, 2019).
A general term for a closed-contour bathymetric low. DEPRESSIONS vary in scale from small local features to larger basins. They generally have lower gradient sides than HOLES.

A general term for an elongated bathymetric low (adapted from IHO, 2019). CHANNELS tend to have flatter and lower gradient FLOORS than GULLIES and CANYONS, and usually have more variable cross-sections than TROUGHS.
A generally broad, planar surface that is at least partially elevated, and lower gradient, than the surrounding areas (adapted from Goudie, 2014).

A generally closed-contoured, relatively flat-topped bathymetric high with one or more relatively steep sides (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "plateau" in Harris et al., (2014).

A generally elongated and planar feature consisting of an upper planar surface, and relatively steeper descending slope. The planar surface is bounded on one side by a steeper ascending slope (modified from IHO, 2019). A TERRACE is not closed-contoured like a PLATEAU, and its lower-gradient surface can be mapped as a PLATFORM.
Cf "terrace" in Harris et al., (2014).

A gently dipping surface, occurring at the base of a bathymetric high, that is elevated relative to the adjacent seafloor.
Cf. "fan/apron" in Harris et al., (2014).
A Geothermal hole is drilled specifically for the exploration, development, appraisal or monitoring of geothermal plays or resources
A line of highest elevation along a bathymetric high, the lateral position of which can vary longitudinally. CRESTS contrast with CENTRELINES, which define mid-line axes along elongated features.

A line of lowest elevation along an elongate bathymetric low, the lateral position of which can vary longitudinally. THALWEGS contrast with CENTRELINES, which define mid-line axes along bathymetric lows.

A line that represents the mid-line long axis of a feature. Contrast CENTRELINE with THALWEG and CREST, which define the lines of lowest and highest elevation, respectively, along an elongate feature.
A linear to curvilinear topographic feature of positive, negative or indeterminate relief. May be discontinuous with variable relief (positive to negative) along its length.
A long, deep, asymmetrical bathymetric low with relatively steep sides (adapted from IHO, 2019). Unlike TROUGHS, TRENCHS are typically asymmetric in cross-section.
A marked and/or abrupt change in slope. BREAK IN SLOPE can be used to define the limit of other features (e.g. SHELF, PLATFORM, ESCARPMENT).
A Mineral hole is drilled for the exploration, development or resource definition of mineral commodities
A narrow break in an elevated feature (modified from IHO, 2019). GAPS are generally narrower than SADDLES.
A Petroleum Well is drilled for the exploration, development or appraisal of hydrocarbons (oil or gas)

A prominent feature rising more than 1000 m above the surrounding relief (modified from IHO, 2019). SEAMOUNTS are larger than KNOLLS and HILLS (>1000 m) and may incorporate PEAKS (and other features).
Cf. "seamount" and "abyss-mountains" in Harris et al., (2014).
A prominent, commonly pointed elevation rising from a larger feature (modified from IHO, 2019).
A protrusion extending from a bathymetric high into deeper water (adapted from IHO, 2019).
A public record of the extent, value, and ownership of land within a district for purposes of taxation.
A relatively broad and lower gradient feature within a bathymetric low, flanked by higher and steeper gradient features.

A relatively shallow elevated feature between (or adjacent to) bathymetric lows (e.g. DEPRESSIONS) (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "sill" in Harris et al., (2014).
A small KNOLL or MOUND, which commonly occurs in groups. HUMMOCKS are generally smaller than KNOLLS and HILLS.
A spire-shaped pillar, either isolated or rising from a larger feature (IHO, 2019).

A steep slope, separating areas of relatively lower slope (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf. "escarpment" in Harris et al., (2014).
A steep-sided, low sinuosity, relatively high-gradient channel. GULLIES are typically smaller than CANYONS.
A Stratigraphic hole is drilled to obtain a record of the Stratigraphic character, composition or property of the rock/soil/regolith units penetrated
A sub-circular (in planform) bathymetric low with steep walls and a generally flat Floor (modified from IHO, 2019). HOLES generally have steeper sides than DEPRESSIONS.
A topographic high of generally conical shape, which may have a truncated peak. CONES have more pointed peaks and are more symmetrical than KNOLLS.

A typically elongated, steep-sided bathymetric low that generally deepens down-slope (modified from IHO, 2019). CANYONS tend to be higher gradient and more complex features than CHANNELS and are larger and more sinuous than GULLIES. VALLEYS tend to have a more limited depth range than CANYONS.
A Water Bore is drilled specifically for the exploration, extraction or monitoring of groundwater resources
Activities relating to production wells, etc. For example logging activities, recompletion, testing, repairs, maintenance, plug and abandonment and demobilisation.
AEM, ground EM, Point located data, grids, derivative grids, algorithms, images, sections, volumes
Age approximations of earth materials such as rocks, sediments and minerals. Age data resulting from scientific techniques such as radio isotopes and chemical dating.

All operations and activities are terminated. The operator has left the site with no intention of returning to the borehole. The borehole is not necessarily plugged.  Similar to CoalLog status "completed".

An abandoned borehole that has also been plugged. The extent of seal may vary from just plugged near the surface (eg, many coal boreholes) to plugged to prevent the migration of oil, gas, water, or other substance from one stratum to another (ie, PPDM definition).
An annular or partially annular bathymetric low typically located at the base of isolated raised features (modified from IHO, 2019).

An Anthropogenic feature to facilitate cultural, societal or religious activities eg cemeteries, recreational areas, land uses related to primary industries,  building infrastructure etc. Excludes information relating to transportation.
An earthquake is the shaking and vibration at the surface of the earth caused by underground movement along a fault plane or by volcanic activity.
An elevated feature which expands (and typically descends) from a locus to a commonly curved outer margin.
An elevation of the seafloor, often found in water depths less than 200 m (adapted from IHO, 2019). BANKS are lower than SEAMOUNTS and tend to occur in shallower water.

An elongated bathymetric low, generally wide and flat bottomed with symmetrical and sub-parallel sides (modified from IHO, 2019). Unlike DEPRESSIONS, TROUGHS are not necessarily closed-contoured, and unlike VALLEYS, they do not always deepen down-slope. Unlike TRENCHS, TROUGHS are typically symmetrical in cross-section.
Cf. "trough" in Harris et al., (2014).

An elongated bathymetric low, typically occurring between prominent bathymetric highs, which generally widens and deepens down-slope (modified from IHO, 2019). VALLEYS have more limited depth-ranges than CANYONS.

An elongated elevation of varying complexity, size and gradient (length > width) (modified from IHO, 2019).
Cf."bridge" and "ridge" in Harris et al., (2014).
An elongated narrow opening (adapted from Goudie,  2014). Unlike CANYONS, CREVICES do not necessarily deepen downslope, and unlike TROUGHS, they are not flat bottomed.

An elongated, narrow and relatively shallow bathymetric low which commonly occurs in parallel or sub-parallel groups. Unlike GAPS, GROOVES are elongated features, that often occur in groups. GROOVES tend to have higher length/width ratios than CREVICES.
An environment in the geologic past
an equivalent relationship to srv:operatesOn from a Service to a Dataset

Any structure fixed to a terrestrial or marine location to facilitate commercial or daily activities, or the supply of essential services. Examples include processing plants / factories, ports, oil rigs etc. This includes information regarding the facilitation of transporting  people or commodities.
associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification
associated through a common heritage such as produced to a common product specification
Borehole is being used for monitoring parameters such as water level, water quality, fluid pressure.

Borehole operations have been halted for a significant period of time or in a manner that requires significant effort for the resumption of activities. The borehole can be plugged downhole and fluids injected on top of the plug in order to create a temporary seal to prevent hydrocarbon leakage. The surface equipment remains in place. Suspended is never the final status of a borehole.

Clearly defined administrative areas of a political, natural, or regulatory boundary nature, excluding scientific speculative boundaries such as geological boundaries, ecological / ecosystem boundaries etc.  Examples include coastlines (because coastlines define certain administrative boundaries), state boundaries, electoral boundaries, mineral tenement boundaries, petroleum title boundaries and marine protected areas.
common title for a collection of resources. NOTE: Title identifies elements of a series collectively, combined with information about what volumes are available at the source cite.
Contains the sampling locations of features which were the target for a set of observations and/or measurements.
Data describing aquifer properties. Porous, water-bearing layers of sand, gravel, and rock below the earth's surface; reservoirs for groundwater.

Data of the physical characteristics of the earth (crust, mantle, core)  such as gravity, magnetics, radiometrics, and seismic. This also includes information relating to basement and crustal studies.

Data on resource production and/or commodity potential or information. Examples include minerals and petroleum potential, oil and gas fields information, resource and production tonnage and grade information, deposit types and mineralisation styles

Data related to the chemical analyses and composition of earth materials. This includes information related to the chemical analysis of organic material, surficial, groundwater and pore water samples, benthic chemical analysis, and stable isotope studies

Data related to the physical expression of the earths surface either terrestrial or marine. Examples include marine bathymetric information, seafloor expressions (mounts, slopes, shelf, rise) freshwater/estuarine bed profiles and  Digital Elevation Models
Data relating to any infrastructure facilitating the movement of people or goods by motorised means. This includes roads, railways and flight paths, aerodromes etc.
Defined areas for the purposes of oil and mineral exploitation
Describing a substance not derived from a living organism and/or not composed of carbon and hydrogen (a hydrocarbon).
Descriptions and polygon outlines of geological provinces of the Australian continent and surrounding offshore regions.
Dissolved solids" refer to any minerals, salts, metals, cations or anions dissolved in water.

Drilling is completed. Production infrastructure may be installed.  The borehole has not necessarily been abandoned.  Note that the CoalLog "completed" status is more similar to "abandoned" in this vocabulary.
Drilling operations have been temporarily suspended (eg, for weather, maintenance, or other reasons).
Elevation data such as Digital Elevation Models (DEMs).
Environmental events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as salinity, acid-sulphate soils, coastal erosion, desertification, climate change
Examples include potentiometric and phreatic surfaces
Examples include vibrating truck and explosives.
External datasets sourced from other Agencies
For example, land vegetation - pest and native flora and fauna
Geological datasets developed for a specific purpose, such as "Volcanoes of the World"
Hydraulic conductivity, porosity, permeability, specific discharge

Hydroacoustic sensing involves "passive acoustics" (listening for sounds) or active acoustics making a sound and listening for the echo, hence the common name for the device, echo sounder or echosounder.
Includes groundwater, porewater and surface water
Includes mineral occurrences, resources, deposits, mineral potential, mineral deposit models, prospectivity maps
Includes seismic, gravity and airborne survey locations. For example, seismic navigation data.
Includes sub-surface distribution of metamorphic, sedimentary and igneous rocks and interpreted linear structural features such as folds, faults, lineaments and shearzones
Includes thin section, fluid inclusion observations and measurements of hand specimens from the surface or down boreholes
Including ground, airborne, point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images

Information regarding the location, purpose and methodology related to particular surveys of any kind. This includes, for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams. This category can be summarised as metadata regarding any type of survey undertaken or managed within the Organisation.
Information related to the distribution,  mapping and physical characteristics of surficial waters. Examples include the locations of bores, lakes, dams, canals, sea levels etc,

Information related to the interpretation of  baseline geoscientific information integrated with field and petrographic observations and measurements. Examples include the interpretation of geological provinces, outcrop and basement geology, 3D geology models, geophysical imagery stratigraphic cross-sections.
Information relating to the supply or provision of utility services such as electricity, gas, steam, water or sewage. Examples include oil , gas , electricity and water pipelines.

Infrasound is sound that is lower in frequency than 20 cycles per second, the normal limit of human hearing. The study of such sound waves is sometimes referred to as infrasonics, covering sounds beneath 20 Hz down to 0.001 Hz. This frequency range is utilized for monitoring earthquakes, charting rock and petroleum formations below the earth

Interpretation of the nature and distribution of outcropping (or near-outcropping) rock units and structures, typically in the form of maps.  These maps may include integrated regolith unit information in areas of non-outcropping bedrock.
Interpretation using geophysical techniques and data

inverse of operatesOn where the domain resource is, or can be, operated on (i.e.queried) by the range resource which must be an agent (i.e. is something that bears some form of responsibility for the operation taking place)
inverse of wasDerivedFrom where the range resource was derived from the domain resource

inverse of wasGeneratedBy where the domain resource generated the range resource implying that the domain resource is a temporal event, such as a data processing action, and the range resource is an entity
Legal boundaries like mining tenements and cadastre

LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) is an optical remote sensing technology that measures properties of scattered light to find range and/or other information of a distant target. The prevalent method to determine distance to an object or surface is to use laser pulses. Like the similar radar technology, which uses radio waves instead of light, the range to an object is determined by measuring the time delay between transmission of a pulse and detection of the reflected signal.

Location determined using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.
Location estimated from a 9 second resolution digital elevation model or image
Location estimated from a 90 metre resolution digital elevation model or image of data from the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission
Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation
Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes
Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and differential GPS
Location estimated from a dead reckoning observation tied to transit satellite fixes and stand alone GPS
Location estimated from a digital elevation model or image

Location estimated from a Global Positioning System prior to May 2000 before when the US government commonly degraded the accuracy of GPS signals by adding intentional, time varying errors of up to 100 meters to the publicly available navigation signal.
Location estimated from a Global Positioning System satellite navigation system
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:1 000 000 scale TEST
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:100 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:25 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:250 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:50 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed map at 1:500 000 scale
Location estimated from a printed topographic, geological, or other map
Location estimated from a satellite image
Location estimated from a satellite image provided by Google Earth or Google Maps
Location estimated from an aerial photograph geometrically corrected ("orthorectified") such that the scale across the image is uniform
Location estimated from an aerial photograph that has not been rectified to a known geographical datum or projection
Location estimated from an orthophoto at 1:25 000 scale
Location estimated from averaging a number of Global Positioning System observations
Location estimated from GPS observation with P-Code access
Location estimated from satellite Doppler and AVLF radio navigation

Location estimated using DGPS, an enhancement to Global Positioning System which uses a network of fixed, ground-based reference stations to broadcast the difference between the positions indicated by the satellite systems and the known fixed positions.
Location estimated using survey from ground control
Location is estimated from an astronomical observation
Location is estimated from an astronomical observation and dead reckoning between fixes.
Magnetic Susceptibility is the degree of magnetization of a material in response to an applied magnetic field.

Manufacturing is the transformation, by means of a tool and/or processing medium, of raw materials into finished goods for sale, or intermediate processes involving the production or finishing of semi-manufactures. It is a large branch of industry and of secondary production.
Material  derived from living organisms and is made up of carbon-based compounds.
Measurements and representation of the earth, its gravitational field and geodynamic phenomena (polar motion, earth tides, and crustal motion) in three-dimensional time varying space.
Measurements of ocean depth and the charting of the topography of the ocean floor.
Measurements taken at a field site such as structural, spectral (PIMA) or geophysical (magnetic susceptibility)

Natural  and human environmental phenomena, whether slow or fast acting.  Information on the natural environment and its interaction with geology, eg waste disposal and pollution such as geological disposal of CO2.

Optical remote sensing makes use of visible, near infrared and short-waveinfrared sensors to form images of the earth's surface by detecting thesolar radiation reflected from targets on the ground. Different materials reflect and absorb differently at different wavelengths. Thus, the targets can be differentiated by their spectral reflectance signatures in the remotely sensed images. Optical remote sensing systems are classified into the following types, depending on the number of spectral bands used in the imaging process.
Palinspastic reconstructions or geological time-slice interpretations
part of a set of imagery that when used together, provides three-dimensional images
part of same structured set of data held in a computer
Passive geophysical surveys are ones that incorporate measurements of naturally occurring fields or properties of the earth.
Pertains to both marine and freshwater ecosystems. For example, reefs
Petrographic observations and measurements such as mineralogy, lithology, alteration, mineralisation, metamorphic grade, weathering and grainsize in specimens and thin sections (micro scale).

Photographs of a part of the earth's surface taken by a camera mounted in an aircraft for mapping purposes. This usually consists of a series of overlapping vertical photos taken in strips which can form the basis for mapping.

Physical measurements of rock properties such as density and susceptibility for use in predictive modelling studies such as seismic predictions, geophysical inversions, and groundwater distribution, or engineering work.
Point located data, grids, derivative grids (filtered, etc), algorithms, images
Point located data, grids, derivative grids (reduced to pole, upward continued, etc), algorithms, images

Porosity and Permeability are two of the primary factors that control the movement and storage of fluids in rocks and sediments. They are intrinsic characteristics of these geologic materials. The exploitation of natural resources, such as groundwater and petroleum, is partly dependent on the properties of porosity and permeability.

RADAR stands for "RAdio Detection And Ranging". By virtue of sending out pulses of microwave electromagnetic radiation this type of instrument can be classified as an "active sensor" - it measures the time between pulses and their reflected components to determine distance. Different pulse intervals, different wavelengths, different geometry and polarizations can be combined to roughness characteristics of the earth surface.
reference from one resource to another - TEST
reference to a master resource of which this one is a part
reference to resources that are parts of this resource
Reflected waves can be generated from a planar boundary in seismic wave speed that can be recorded at the Earth's surface
Remote sensing of direct temperature effects is carried out by sensing radiation emitted from matter in the thermal infrared region of the spectrum.
resource is a revision of associated resource

Satellite and air platform imagery. This includes imagery designed to represent surficial condition of the earth such as Landsat, SPOT, MODIS, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography. Remotely sensed data acquired from earth observation satellites and Sensing Instruments for both scientific and operational applications, such as LANDSAT 5,LANDSAT 7,RADARSAT 1 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR), ERS 2 (Synthetic Aperture Radar - SAR) TERRA Modis,AQUA Modis, NOAA 17, SPOT, ASTER, HYMAP and Aerial Photography
SEGY, seismic profiles, images, processed data, weak and strong motion from seismographs and accelerators
Similar data to other geophysical types, but in the "spectral geology" space, with many purposes. For example, PIMA and Hylogger
Stratigraphy based on the paleontologic aspects of rocks.
Sudden emergency events associated with acts of terrorism causing damage to human life or the economy
Sudden events associated with earth processes that impact Australian communities such as earthquake, landslide, flood, tsunami, wildfire, tropical cyclone, storm surge, severe windstorm
The benthic zone is the ecological region at the lowest level of a body of water such as an ocean or a lake, including the sediment surface and some sub-surface layers.

The borehole has been filled or sealed. The extent of seal may vary from just plugged near the surface (eg, many coal boreholes) to plugged in order to prevent the migration of oil, gas, water, or other substance from one stratum to another (ie, PPDM definition).
The borehole site has been fully rehabilitated. Note that this status is included within the PPDM definition of "plugged and abandoned".
The climate of a given interval of time in the the geologic past.
The density of a material is defined as its mass per unit volume.
The distribution and movement of groundwater and dissolved materials in the soil and rocks of the Earth's crust (commonly in aquifers).

The distribution of plants, animals; natural biotic distribution patterns and habitats for aerial, terrestrial, marine, and other life.BIOME: are distinct ecological communities of plants and animals living together in a particular climate. Scientists classify biomes in various ways, but the major biomes include aquatic, deserts, forests, grasslands, and tundra.?
the domain resource was derived from the range resource which must be an entity (i.e. not an actor with agency or a temporal event)
the domain resource was generated by the range resource which must have been a temporal event such as a data processing action or a field survey

The domain resource was informed by the range resource which must have been an Entity (i.e. not an Agent with agency or an Activity temporal event). This is a looser association than wasDerivedFrom and does not indicate direct derivation, as wasDerivedFrom does.
The drilling status of the borehole is unknown.
The location is supplied by a private company. The location method is not known.
The location is supplied by a State or Territory government agency. The location method is not known.
The location is supplied via personal communication.  The location method is not known.
The location is taken from a published report or article.  The location method is not known.
The location is taken from an unpublished report.  The location method is not known.
The location method is known, but is not specified in this vocabulary
The location method is unknown. However, the correct value probably exists.
The recorded date is the earliest known date, but it is possible the true date is earlier than what is recorded.
The recorded date is the latest known date, but it is possible the true date is later than what is recorded.
The shapes, patterns and physical configuration of the surface of the land, including its relief (local differences in elevation) and the positions of natural and man-made features.
The status of a borehole commencing from the rig being moved onsite to the time that drilling operations are completed.  Similar to PPDM's "drilling", but different.
The status of a borehole during  which liquids or gas are being extracted from the borehole.
The status of a borehole during which liquids or gas are being pumped into the borehole.
The status of a borehole that alternately produces and injects liquid or gas into a borehole.
The study of life in past geologic time, based on fossil plants and animals. Chronology of the Earth's history.

The study of the origin of landscapes based on a cause and effect relationship. This includes the physical and chemical interactions between the earth's surface and the natural forces acting on it, such as geology, climate, vegetation and relative base level.
The theory and practice of classifying plants and animals.
The use of non-radiogenic dating studies such as U-Pb ratios
Thermal properties of fluids, gases and solids
This includes for example navigational data for seismic surveys, landsat path row diagrams and field mapping survey and site information including field geology observations and measurements.
Visual observations and photos of the vegetation, landscape and geomorphology at a field site and descriptions of the mineralogy, lithology, alteration of rocks, samples and outcrops at a field site.
Zones of similar mineralogy caused by heat and pressure
